title: SEGMENT 12 — Disease in a dish: modeling Parkinson's, ALS, and diabetes

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Disease in a dish: modeling Parkinson's, ALS, and diabetes
One of the most powerful applications of iPSCs is something the field calls disease modeling, or more colorfully, "disease in a dish.
" Here's the idea.
You have a patient with a genetic disease.
Parkinson's caused by a mutation in LRRK2.
ALS caused by a mutation in SOD1.
Type 1 diabetes — though that's not strictly genetic, the autoimmune aspect makes it harder, but bear with me.
You want to study what's wrong with this patient's affected cells — the dopamine neurons, the motor neurons, the beta cells.
But you can't biopsy a living person's brain or pancreas to get them.

So historically, you've been limited to postmortem tissue (dead cells with all sorts of artifacts), animal models (mice are not humans), or cell lines that don't have the patient's genetic background.
With iPSCs, you can do something new.
You take a skin biopsy from the patient — a few square millimeters of fibroblasts.
You reprogram those fibroblasts to iPSCs.
The iPSCs carry the patient's exact genetic background, including the disease-causing mutation.
Then you differentiate the iPSCs into the cell type that's affected by the disease — dopamine neurons, motor neurons, beta cells, whatever.
You now have, in a dish, a population of cells that share the patient's genetics and represent the affected cell type.
You can study them.
You can watch them die.
You can compare them to cells from healthy controls.
You can test drugs on them.
The Parkinson's application has been particularly fruitful.
Researchers take skin from patients with familial Parkinson's — patients carrying mutations in LRRK2, SNCA, GBA1, or PARK2 — reprogram to iPSCs, and differentiate to midbrain dopamine neurons.
The resulting neurons show Parkinson's-like features: accumulation of misfolded alpha-synuclein, mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, eventually cell death.
You can watch a Parkinson's neuron die in your dish, over weeks.
You can then test compounds that might slow or prevent the death.
That's an enormously powerful screening platform.
The ALS application is similar.
Motor neurons derived from iPSCs of ALS patients — particularly those with mutations in SOD1, C9ORF72, or TDP-43 — show ALS-like features in the dish.
Mislocalization of TDP-43.
Aggregation of toxic proteins.
Impaired axonal transport.
Eventually, death.
Researchers screen compounds that protect these motor neurons, hoping to find drugs that might slow the disease in patients.
Several ALS clinical trials of drugs identified through iPSC screening are ongoing.
For type 1 diabetes — where the immune system destroys the patient's beta cells — iPSC-derived beta cells are useful both for studying the disease and as a potential source of replacement cells.
You can derive iPSCs from a diabetic patient, differentiate to beta cells, and study why those beta cells are autoimmune targets.
Or, more practically, you can derive beta cells from anyone and use them as transplantable replacement tissue, which is the territory we'll cover in the next segment.
A particularly elegant variant of disease modeling is CRISPR-corrected isogenic controls.
Here's the setup.
You have iPSCs from a patient carrying a Parkinson's-causing mutation.
You also want a control — cells from a healthy person — to compare them to.
But any healthy control will differ from your patient in millions of other genetic variants, not just the disease mutation.
How do you know which differences in the dish are caused by the disease mutation and which are caused by all the other genetic noise?
Answer: use CRISPR to fix the disease mutation in the patient's own iPSCs.
Now you have two cell lines that are genetically identical except at the one nucleotide of the disease mutation.
The patient line, with the mutation, and the corrected line, without it.
Any difference in behavior between the two has to be caused by that single nucleotide change.
You've controlled for genetic background completely.
This is called an isogenic control, and it's now standard practice in iPSC disease modeling.
It dramatically sharpens what you can conclude from your experiments.
iPSC disease modeling, in some ways, has been the most quietly productive application of the technology.
It doesn't make news the way clinical trials do.
But it has produced thousands of papers, illuminated dozens of disease mechanisms, and contributed to drug development pipelines across the industry.
Every major pharma company has an iPSC group.
The technology has become infrastructure.
